plainMeaning: This verse bows to six of the twenty Tīrthaṅkaras living right now in Mahāvideha — Lord Svayamprabha (the sixth), Ṛṣabhānana, Anantavīrya, Sūraprabha, Viśāla and Vajradhara (the eleventh).
recitation: chaṭṭho sayaṁpaha pahu, usabhāṇaṇa taha aṇaṁtavirio a; sūrappaho visālo, vajjadharo taha igārasamo.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit chaṭṭha < Sanskrit ṣaṣṭha ('sixth'); ordinal of chha/ṣaṣ ('six')
wordByWord.notes: Counting marker fixing Svayamprabha as no. 6 in the list of 20 viharamāṇa Jinas.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit Svayaṁprabha (svayam 'of itself' + prabhā 'radiance, light')
wordByWord.notes: Proper name of the 6th living Tīrthaṅkara of Mahāvideha.
Honorific attached to Svayamprabha; carries the reverential force of the whole roll-call.
The 7th viharamāṇa Jina; the name echoes the bull-emblem associated with the line of Ṛṣabha.
Connective stringing the next name onto the garland; first of two occurrences in this verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit Anantavīrya (ananta 'endless' + vīrya 'valour, energy, potency')
Prakrit a < Sanskrit ca ('and'), a coordinating enclitic
wordByWord.notes: Light metrical 'and' closing the first half-line.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit < Sanskrit Sūryaprabha / Sūraprabha (sūra/sūrya 'sun' + prabhā 'radiance'); the doubled -pp- is regular Prakrit assimilation
wordByWord.notes: The 9th viharamāṇa Jina; pairs in imagery with Svayamprabha (self-light) earlier in the verse.

wordByWord.gloss: Viśāla ('the vast / expansive one'), i.e. Viśāladhara
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit visāla < Sanskrit viśāla ('broad, vast, ample')
wordByWord.notes: The 10th viharamāṇa Jina; standard lists give the fuller form Viśāladhara, here metrically shortened to Viśāla.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vajja < Sanskrit vajra ('thunderbolt, adamant') + dhara ('holder, bearer'); -jj- is the Prakrit reflex of -jr-

wordByWord.notes: Second occurrence; here it leans on the following ordinal to mean 'and thus (the eleventh)'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit igārasama < Sanskrit ekādaśama ('eleventh'); ordinal of igāraha/ekādaśa ('eleven')
wordByWord.notes: Closing counting marker fixing Vajradhara as no. 11, bracketing the verse with chaṭṭho (6th).

literalTranslation: The sixth, Lord Svayamprabha; Ṛṣabhānana, and likewise Anantavīrya; Sūraprabha, Viśāla(dhara), and Vajradhara — thus the eleventh.
idiomaticTranslation: I bow to the sixth, Lord Svayamprabha, and to Ṛṣabhānana, and so too to Anantavīrya; to Sūraprabha, to Viśāladhara, and to Vajradhara — and with him the eleventh is told.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This gāthā is the middle bead of the viharamāṇa-string, carrying the count forward from the previous verse — which named the first five, Sīmandhara through Sujāta — to the next, which will finish the twenty from Candrānana onward. Its art lies in the two ordinal pins, chaṭṭho ('sixth') opening the verse and igārasamo ('eleventh') sealing it: between them sit exactly six names, so the reciter can never lose his place in the roll of twenty. Notice the deliberate music of the names. Two are built on light — Svayamprabha, 'self-luminous,' and Sūraprabha, 'sun-bright' — framing the verse in radiance; one on boundless power, Anantavīrya; one on cosmic adamant, Vajradhara, 'thunderbolt-bearer'; one on vastness, Viśāla(dhara); and Ṛṣabhānana, 'bull-faced,' whose very name recalls the bull-emblem of the first Jina Ṛṣabha. The honorific pahu ('Lord') on Svayamprabha and the connectives taha…a ('and…likewise') keep the line a continuous act of homage rather than a bare list. Where most of the hymn salutes Jinas of the past, the eternal, or the historically founded shrines, this stretch is unique in bowing to omniscients who are alive and teaching at this very moment — a fact the bare names quietly carry without elaboration.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The twenty viharamāṇa Jinas embody the Jain conviction that the dharma-tīrtha is never wholly extinguished in the universe: while the five Bharata and Airāvata fields pass through Jina-less down-cycles (the present fifth ara among them), the Mahāvideha fields abide perpetually in a fourth-ara-like condition where omniscient Tīrthaṅkaras are continuously present and preaching. Tradition holds a minimum of twenty such living Jinas at all times (rising to as many as 170), so a kevalī's voice is always sounding somewhere. Bowing to Svayamprabha, Anantavīrya, Vajradhara and their fellows is therefore homage to living, embodied omniscience (sayoga-kevalī), distinct from the salutation of liberated or eternal Jinas elsewhere in the hymn — affirming that the path remains open and currently staffed.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For a reciter standing in the Jina-less fifth ara of Bharata, this verse is a lifeline: it lets one take darśana, in words, of Tīrthaṅkaras who are awake and teaching right now. Pronouncing each name by its number — sixth … eleventh — trains devotional precision, so the practitioner honours each living Jina individually rather than as a blur, renewing the felt link to a presently active source of the path.

notes: The verse names viharamāṇa Jinas nos. 6–11; the ordinals chaṭṭho (6th) and igārasamo (11th) explicitly fix the positions and confirm the identifications. 'Visālo' (no. 10) is the metrically shortened form of the standard Viśāladhara found in the canonical lists. Spellings here follow the book's Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa orthography (e.g. sayaṁpaha, vajjadharo, igārasamo) and are not normalised. Name-forms vary slightly across editions (Surprabh/Sūraprabha; Ṛṣabhānana/Rishabhanan); meanings are stable. The doublet taha occurs twice and is glossed at each occurrence for full token coverage.
